epigraph.text:
For everything there is a season, and a time for 
every matter under heaven.
epigraph.quote: Logic is the beginning of wisdom, not the end.

content.text: {Logic is the beginning of wisdom, not the end.}{Leonard Nimoy}

We begin with the fundamental distinction between relations and 
functions. While both describe connections between quantities, 
functions obey a special rule that makes them particularly useful.

content.title: Relation
content.content:
A **relation** is any set of ordered pairs. The 
set of all first coordinates is the **domain**; the set of all 
second coordinates is the **range**.

content.title: Identifying domain and range
content.problem:
Find the domain and range of the relation:
\[
 \{(1, 4), (2, 5), (3, 4), (4, 7)\}
\]
content.solution:
Domain $= \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ (all first coordinates)

Range $= \{4, 5, 7\}$ (all second coordinates, no repeats)

content.title: Function
content.content:
A **function** is a relation in which each input 
(each element of the domain) is paired with **exactly one** output.

content.text: The key requirement: no input can have two different outputs.

content.title: Function or not?
content.solution:
1. **Function**: Each input has exactly one output.

2. **Not a function**: Input $1$ is paired with both $2$ and $6$.

3. **Function**: Different inputs can have the same output; 
 that's allowed. What matters is that each *input* has only 
 one output.

content.text:
A **mapping diagram** shows the pairing visually with arrows from 
inputs to outputs.
For graphs, we have a visual test:

content.title: Vertical Line Test
content.content:
A graph represents a function if and only if no vertical line 
intersects the graph more than once.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Using the vertical line test

content.text:
Think of a function as a machine: you put something in (input), the 
machine processes it according to its rule, and something comes out 
(output). The same input always produces the same output.

marginNotes.text:
In the beginning God created the heavens 
and the earth.

marginNotes.author: Gottfried Leibniz
marginNotes.text:
Nothing is more important than to see 
the sources of invention which are more interesting than the inventions 
themselves.
